current_fact.fact_text: In 1952, Japan and the Republic of China signed the Treaty of Taipei, formally ending the Second Sino-Japanese War between them. The agreement was part of a wider postwar rearrangement in East Asia, where peace treaties tried to tidy up the lingering shadows of occupation and conflict.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

current_fact.match_reason: After years of violent conflict, a quiet peace treaty mirrors the episode’s theme of slowing down, exhaling, and finding a gentler rhythm after upheaval.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1949, former Philippine First Lady Aurora Quezon was assassinated en route to inaugurate a hospital named for her late husband. Her death at the hands of insurgents turned a symbolic act of charity into a national trauma, and the road she traveled into a fault line of memory.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: The ambush of a figure traveling to open a hospital invites solemn reflection on fragility and interrupted journeys, well matched with an introspective episode about recovery, vulnerability, and looking back at what broke.

queue.fact_text: In 1937, South African-born virologist Max Theiler developed an effective yellow fever vaccine at the Rockefeller Foundation in New York. His work turned a deadly mosquito-borne disease into a preventable illness and earned him the Nobel Prize, showing how a single vaccine can rewrite public health.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: Developing a vaccine required years of meticulous experimentation and learning from failure, perfectly in tune with an episode about the science and psychology of learning, persistence, and gradual breakthroughs.

queue.fact_text: In 1794, Sardinians led by Giovanni Maria Angioy launched a revolt against the House of Savoy, expelling the viceroy from Cagliari. Their uprising mixed local grievances with Enlightenment ideas, turning a Mediterranean island into a stage for revolution and self-determination.

episode.title: Morning Drops #30 - Star Treatment
episode.description: A celebration of change and transformation. Gaia traces the evolution of Arctic Monkeys - from raw energy to cinematic sound - reflecting on how artists, and all of us, grow and change through time. A journey through courage, curiosity, and the beauty of slowing down.
songs: Arctic Monkeys - There’d Better Be a Mirrorball

queue.match_reason: A local revolution against an old dynasty echoes the episode’s theme of transformation and shedding old identities, from monarchy to modernity or from raw garage rock to sophisticated cinematic sound.

queue.fact_text: In 1994, Aldrich Ames, a veteran CIA counterintelligence officer, pleaded guilty to spying for the Soviet Union and later Russia. His leaks compromised dozens of assets and exposed how deeply trust can be corroded from within the very system meant to guard secrets.

episode.title: Sedna FM #06 - Alters of an Ego
episode.description: What if you could step outside yourself and become someone freer, bolder, or completely unexpected? Exploring the artistry of musicians who craft alter-egos and parallel realities to experiment with new dimensions of their identity. It's not about hiding who they are — it's about expanding who they can be.
songs: The Fireman - Strawberries Oceans Ships Forest

queue.match_reason: A spy living a double life is a dark real-world alter ego; the episode’s exploration of multiple identities and constructed personas provides a musical lens on secrecy, masks, and split selves.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1796, Vittorio Amedeo III—Victor Amadeus III—King of Sardinia, signed away territory under pressure from Napoleon, only to die later that same year. His reign closed as the old order of European monarchies started to buckle under revolutionary and imperial forces.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'è Profondo il Mare

queue.match_reason: A Sardinian king’s twilight under revolutionary pressure fits an episode about contrasting Italian energies—quiet fog and eruptive fire—mirroring the fading of one era and the ignition of another.

queue.fact_text: In 1944, during World War II, the E-boat attack on Exercise Tiger turned a training ground into a battlefield; decades earlier in 1914–1918, music was also marshaled for morale—but in 1947, a very different kind of mission left South America by raft. (But here we return to a quieter thread.)

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: Yellow fever’s spread across continents and its eventual control tell a global, atmospheric story; the moody, border-blurring textures of trip-hop provide an apt sonic echo of a disease and cure that moved quietly through the world.

queue.fact_text: In 1935, French virologist Max Theiler’s work on yellow fever set the stage for widespread vaccination campaigns in tropical regions. The victory over the virus turned once-feared ports and jungles into safer routes for trade, travel, and human connection.

episode.title: Morning Drops #23 - Morning Dubs
episode.description: Recorded while still processing Houghton Festival. One of the mornings there, waking up in the woods to a reggae DJ set — no phone signal, just trees, music, and loved ones. A slow selection of reggae, dub, and morning rhythms. Dubs instead of drops. Start your day slow — with space to breathe.
songs: The Gaylads - Joy in the Morning
King Tubby - King Tubby Meets the Rockers Uptown

queue.match_reason: Yellow fever deeply shaped life in tropical and Caribbean regions where reggae and dub later thrived; the relaxed, nature-rooted atmosphere of this episode offers a healing, post-epidemic counterpoint to the disease’s history.

queue.fact_text: In 2001, the first full draft of the human genome was announced complete, but decades before, counterintelligence work like Aldrich Ames’s depended on more analog forms of pattern reading. The web of secrets, betrayals, and shifting loyalties in his case reads almost like a psychological concept album.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: Ames’s intricate double life and the narrative weight of espionage sit well beside an eclectic, storytelling-heavy first episode that feels like a soundtrack to lives lived in parallel—public and hidden.

published.fact_text: In 1991, Space Shuttle Discovery lifted off on mission STS-39, the first unclassified shuttle flight dedicated to the U.S. Department of Defense. Its experiments probed the upper atmosphere and tested new infrared instruments, quietly blending space science with Cold War-era military innovation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A space shuttle mission pairs naturally with an episode themed around 'space' and new beginnings, both inviting listeners to see emptiness as a launchpad for exploration.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1973, Pink Floyd’s The Dark Side of the Moon reached number one on the U.S. Billboard chart, beginning an astonishing 741-week run. The album’s seamless blend of experimental sound, studio wizardry, and existential themes turned a progressive rock record into a long-term cultural orbit.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

published.match_reason: The fact is directly about Pink Floyd’s landmark album, and this episode not only includes a Pink Floyd track but also frames listening as a cosmic voyage, echoing the album’s spacey, conceptual legacy.

published.fact_text: In 1947, Thor Heyerdahl and five crewmates launched the Kon-Tiki, a balsa-wood raft, from Peru to test whether ancient South Americans could have reached Polynesia by sea. For 101 days they let wind and current dictate their path, turning an anthropological hypothesis into a high-risk experiment on the open Pacific.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: An oceanic experiment aboard a fragile raft resonates perfectly with a sailing-themed episode built around life at sea, shared journeys, and the rhythm of waves.

published.fact_text: In 1986, abnormal radiation levels were first detected at Sweden’s Forsmark Nuclear Power Plant, hundreds of kilometers from Chernobyl. The Swedish alarms forced Soviet authorities to admit that a catastrophic nuclear accident had occurred, revealing how invisible particles can betray even tightly held secrets.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: Both the Chernobyl revelation and the episode explore unseen forces — radiation and digital overload — that quietly shape modern life and only become visible when alarms (literal or psychological) go off.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1948, Igor Stravinsky conducted the American premiere of his ballet Orpheus in New York. The work reimagined the ancient myth with cool, neoclassical clarity, turning a timeless story of loss and music into a modern, crystalline soundscape.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: A mythic, neoclassical ballet conducted by Stravinsky aligns with an episode built on cinematic, neo-classical moods, both transforming storytelling into lush, orchestral soundscapes.

published.fact_text: In 1869, Chinese and Irish laborers on the Central Pacific Railroad laid an unprecedented ten miles of track in a single day while building America’s first transcontinental railroad. This feat of coordination and endurance, achieved with hand tools and sheer muscle, has never been matched in railway construction.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

published.match_reason: The record-setting day of track-laying evokes relentless forward motion and travel, mirrored by this high-energy, on-the-move playlist built for journeys and momentum.

published.fact_text: In 1789, a group of sailors aboard HMS Bounty overthrew their captain, William Bligh, and set him and loyal crew adrift in an open boat. Bligh then navigated more than 3,600 nautical miles to safety, an extraordinary feat of leadership and seamanship born from one of history’s most famous mutinies.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

published.match_reason: This episode literally includes a song about sailors (“Ma come fanno i marinai”) and journeys, making it a poetic echo of Bligh’s improbable survival voyage after the Bounty mutiny.

published.fact_text: On this day in 2004, CBS News released photographs from Abu Ghraib prison showing U.S. personnel torturing and abusing Iraqi detainees. The shocking images ignited global outrage and forced a reckoning with how power, war, and technology can combine to expose hidden brutality.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A visceral human-rights scandal ties strongly to an episode about one of the most politically confrontational bands, echoing themes of state violence, protest, and accountability.

published.fact_text: In 1944, during Exercise Tiger—rehearsals for the D-Day landings—German E-boats attacked Allied landing ships off the English coast, killing 946 servicemen. The disaster was kept so secret that some survivors later went into the actual Normandy invasion carrying the memory of a deadly rehearsal no one was allowed to mention.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: The sudden, deadly attack on training forces feels like a literal 'electric shock' in wartime history, resonating with the episode’s sharp, high-voltage rock energy and themes of risk and intensity.

published.fact_text: In 1625, a joint Spanish–Portuguese fleet of 52 ships began the recapture of Bahia in Brazil from the Dutch. The massive operation showed how colonial rivalries could transform distant harbors into chessboards for empires and their ocean-spanning ambitions.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A vast seaborne campaign between European empires played out on Latin American shores connects with an episode steeped in Latin folk and world-travel moods, reframing conquest-era oceans as places of movement and perspective.

published.fact_text: In 1996, amid the Whitewater controversy, U.S. President Bill Clinton gave a 4½-hour videotaped testimony for the defense. It was a moment when a sitting president’s legal and political worlds collided under the unblinking eye of the camera.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

published.match_reason: A long, scrutinized presidential testimony embodies the discomfort of re-examining narratives and defending choices, which dovetails with an episode explicitly about unlearning, doubt, and changing one’s mind.

published.fact_text: In 1965, U.S. troops landed in the Dominican Republic, officially to prevent a communist takeover during the Dominican Civil War. The intervention highlighted how Cold War anxieties could redraw the lines between domestic revolution and foreign occupation overnight.

episode.title: Morning Drops #13 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

published.match_reason: A U.S. intervention in the Caribbean connects to an episode unpacking a major Caribbean-born genre; both speak to how politics, power, and culture intertwine across this region.

published.fact_text: In 1941, the Ustaše regime carried out the Gudovac massacre, killing nearly 200 Serbs in one of the earliest genocidal attacks in the Independent State of Croatia. It marked the beginning of a brutal campaign of ethnic persecution that would scar the Balkans for generations.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

published.match_reason: Facing the memory of genocide requires voices that refuse to be silenced; this episode’s celebration of defiant, boundary-breaking women in rock mirrors the need to confront and shout against fascist violence.

published.fact_text: In 1796, the Armistice of Cherasco was signed between Napoleon Bonaparte and King Victor Amadeus III of Sardinia, forcing Sardinia to cede key fortresses and coastline to France. The agreement quietly reshaped the political geography of the Mediterranean as Napoleon’s star was rising.

episode.title: Morning Drops #18 - Sardegna
episode.description: Yasmin traces her connection to Sardegna — the island where her Italian journey began. Through music and memory, she shares the story of how this place shaped her family's path, and why it still holds such a deep place in her heart.
songs: Mahmood & Tenores di Bitti - Com’è profondo il mare

published.match_reason: The armistice directly involved the Kingdom of Sardinia and altered Sardinian history; an episode devoted to Sardegna’s identity and memory is an ideal musical counterpoint to that political turning point.

published.fact_text: In 1975, as North Vietnamese forces closed in on Saigon, General Cao Văn Viên, the chief of South Vietnam’s military, departed for the United States. His flight symbolized the collapse of a state and the human dimension of wars that end not with a line on a map, but with departures and exile.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

published.match_reason: A general leaving as his country falls encapsulates an ending and uncertain new chapter, fitting an episode about transitions, storms passing, and holding onto hope through change.

published.fact_text: In 1952, Japan and the Republic of China signed the Treaty of Taipei, formally ending the Second Sino-Japanese War between them. The agreement was part of a wider postwar rearrangement in East Asia, where peace treaties tried to tidy up the lingering shadows of occupation and conflict.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: After years of violent conflict, a quiet peace treaty mirrors the episode’s theme of slowing down, exhaling, and finding a gentler rhythm after upheaval.
